text: Literally, this verse appears to imply that Juda has gone into exilebecause of affliction; and some think the reference is to refugee Jews in Egypt and elsewhere. But the word used for ‘exile’ really means ‘deportation’.

Literally, ‘her enemies derided her sabbath’, that is, her quiescence. The Hebrew text is generally interpreted as meaning that Sion, in her affliction, remembers her past glories; but the expression is obscure.
‘An outlaw’; literally, ‘unable to hold her ground’; but the word used in the Hebrew text implies ceremonial defilement.

The opening of this verse, in the Hebrew text, is of uncertain interpretation; some think the meaning is, ‘May it never happen to you!’ The vineyard metaphor used here and in verse 22 seems due to a mistaken interpretation in the Latin.
Literally, ‘for I am full of bitterness’. But the Hebrew text has, ‘for truth it is that I have rebelled against thee’.
